schools.summary: The law had 'a shadow of good things to come' (skian, a faint outline foreshadowing Christ's blessings) 'and not the very image' (eikon, the solid substance; Christ the image of God, Col 2:17); so its sacrifices 'offered year by year' could never 'make the comers perfect' (cleanse the conscience); the shadow cannot do the work of the substance, its repetition betraying its weakness.

themes: a shadow of good things to come

schools.summary: The argument from repetition — had the sacrifices truly perfected, 'would they not have ceased to be offered?' (a final cleansing ending the need); the test 'the worshippers once purged should have had no more conscience of sins' (no lingering guilt); their endless repeating the very confession of failure, a standing testimony to imperfection (Ellicott).

The yearly sacrifice (the day of atonement) made 'a remembrance' (anamnesis, a reminder) of sins year by year — far from removing sin, a renewed confession that it stood unremoved (the high priest's threefold acknowledgment, Ellicott); the opposite of true atonement, which puts sin out of remembrance (v17); a clear proof guilt was not purged (Wesley).

The decisive reason — 'it is not possible' (utterly, not merely insufficient) that the blood of bulls and goats 'take away sins' (aphairein); common sense itself showing animal blood could not satisfy justice or cleanse the conscience (Clarke); yet the offerings divinely appointed and necessary in their season, pointing beyond themselves — a symbol and pledge of the real cleansing in Christ (Calvin).

Christ at his Incarnation ('when he cometh into the world') quoting Ps 40:6-8 — 'Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not' (God never willed the legal sacrifices to atone) 'but a body hast thou prepared me' (a human body to be offered); the Hebrew reads 'ears hast thou digged,' the LXX (followed here) 'a body' — the opened ear of obedience and the prepared body both pointing to Christ's willing self-offering.

debates.question: Why does the text read 'a body' where the Hebrew Psalm has 'ears'?

sides.position: the writer follows the LXX; the sense is consonant — the opened ear of obedience and the prepared body of the obedient Servant both point to Christ's willing self-offering

themes: when he cometh into the world
a body hast thou prepared me

schools.summary: The divine verdict on the whole range of sacrifices — the slain offering, meal-offering, 'whole burnt offerings' (the symbol of entire consecration), 'sacrifices for sin' — 'thou hast had no pleasure'; God finding no satisfaction in any as the ground of atonement (they could not satisfy his justice, Clarke), not valueless in their typical place but inadequate to remove sin; his pleasure lay in the obedience of his Son.

themes: burnt offerings and sacrifices for sin

schools.summary: The climax — when the sacrifices could not please God, the Son says 'Lo, I come'; 'in the volume of the book' (the scroll/roll of Scripture) 'it is written of me' (his coming fulfilling the written word); 'to do thy will, O God' (genitive of purpose) — his obedient self-offering replacing the legal offerings; he comes to do God's will by the sacrifice of himself (Wesley).

themes: in the volume of the book

schools.summary: The writer's exposition, first stage — recalling the Psalm's opening, 'Sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not,' with the pointed 'which are offered by the law' (Israel's own Scripture declaring God's dissatisfaction with the law's offerings); answering the objection why the types continued after the truth came (Chrysostom) — the Psalm foretold their setting aside; the negative side establishing that the old is removed.

The great conclusion — the Son's coming 'to do thy will' means 'he taketh away the first' (anairei, abolishes the legal sacrifices that could not please God) 'that he may establish the second' (the doing of God's will by the offering of Christ's body, once for all, Clarke); the Jews' own Scripture foretelling the abolition of their sacrificial system and its replacement by Christ's obedience (Calvin).

themes: 'Lo, I come to do thy will'

schools.summary: The saving result — 'by the which will' (God's will fulfilled in Christ's self-offering) 'we are sanctified' (hegiasmenoi, perfect, a settled state — cleansed from guilt and consecrated to God) 'through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all' (the prepared body offered in obedient sacrifice, Phil 2:8, not repeated); the one effectual offering against the law's many ineffectual ones, the perfecting the law could not give.

themes: by the which will we are sanctified
the offering of the body of Jesus

schools.summary: The standing priest vs the seated Christ — 'every priest standeth daily' (hesteken, the posture of a servant whose work is never done) 'offering oftentimes the same sacrifices' (endless sameness betraying nothing accomplished) 'which can never take away sins'; the perpetual standing and fruitless offering throwing into relief Christ's once-for-all completed offering; daily sacrificing wholly foreign to Christ's priesthood (Calvin).

The contrast crowned — 'but this man,' against the many standing priests, offered 'one sacrifice for sins' (mian, a single offering doing what the many could not) 'for ever' (avails perpetually), then 'sat down on the right hand of God' (the posture of completion and royal majesty, his atoning work done, as a Son not a labouring servant, Wesley); the seated Christ declaring the atonement finished and accepted.

themes: one sacrifice for sins for ever

schools.summary: The seated Christ 'henceforth expecting' (to loipon ekdechomenos, waiting in the calm assurance of certain victory, 1 Cor 15:25), his redemptive work complete; 'till his enemies be made his footstool' (Ps 110:1, the Messianic promise of reign until every foe is subdued, including all who oppose his priesthood, Clarke); the posture of expectation befitting the finished atonement.

The crowning result — against the law that 'can never make perfect' (v1), Christ 'hath perfected' (teteleioken, perfect, a completed abiding achievement) 'by one offering' (the single self-sacrifice, doing what the many could not) 'for ever' (full and lasting, needing no repetition) 'them that are sanctified'; 'perfected' meaning complete remission of sins and consecration to holiness, the perfection the law could not give.

The Spirit summoned as witness — 'the Holy Ghost also is a witness to us' (the Spirit who spoke through the prophets attesting the finished sacrifice); a high view of Scripture implied — Jeremiah's words (Jer 31:33, quoted again from Heb 8:8) the very witness of the Spirit; the writer winding up his whole argument, the new covenant now fully ratified (Wesley); divine authority for the conclusion.

themes: the Holy Ghost is a witness

schools.summary: The new covenant recited (Jer 31:33, as in Heb 8:10) — 'I will put my laws into their hearts, and in their minds will I write them'; God writing his law not on tablets of stone (external, condemning) but within, so obedience flows from an inward principle of grace; the fruit of the sanctification by Christ's offering, the law inscribed by the Spirit; 'after those days,' the new age replacing the old.

The crowning promise — 'their sins and iniquities will I remember no more' (not merely to overlook but to remember no more, blotting sin from his reckoning); God's non-remembrance of sin precisely its remission (Gill); the sharp contrast with the yearly 'remembrance of sins' (v3) — full pardon replacing perpetual recall; both 'sins' and 'iniquities,' the whole guilt, put away forever.

The triumphant conclusion — 'where remission of these is' (the sins forgiven, remembered no more) 'there is no more offering for sin'; if sin is truly and finally pardoned, no further sacrifice can be needed or possible (a debt fully paid cannot be paid again); overthrowing the rationale of the repeated Levitical sacrifices, Christ's one offering securing full remission; the doctrinal section's triumphant close (Ellicott).

The turn from doctrine to exhortation — 'therefore' (on the ground of the finished sacrifice), 'boldness to enter into the holiest' (the inmost sanctuary, entered under the law only by the high priest once a year, now open to every believer with free confident entrance) 'by the blood of Jesus' (the access resting wholly on his finished work); resuming the whole argument (cf 4:14-16), all ceremonial barriers removed (Calvin).

Two figures — 'a new and living way' ('new,' prosphaton, freshly opened, none having entered the heavenly sanctuary before Christ; 'living,' giving and sustaining life, Christ himself the way), 'consecrated/dedicated for us' (opened by his work); 'through the veil, that is to say, his flesh' (as the temple veil shut off the Holy of Holies, Christ's flesh veiled the glory and, rent in death, opened the way); the Godhead hidden in his flesh leading us into heaven (Calvin).

The second ground of confidence — 'an high priest' (hierea megan, a 'great priest,' surpassing the Levitical, having offered the one perfect sacrifice and entered the heavenly sanctuary, Heb 4:14-7:28) 'over the house of God' (set over the church not as a servant like Moses but as a Son, Heb 3:6, offering his blood and presenting his people's prayers, Clarke); a faithful interceding High Priest completing the assurance of access.

The first 'let us' — 'let us draw near' (proserchometha, the first of three exhortations, Heb 4:16; the access used in worship and communion) 'with a true heart' (sincerity) 'in full assurance of faith' (confident trust resting on Christ); a twofold cleansing — 'hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience' (the inner man cleansed by Christ's blood, the conscience no longer condemning) and 'bodies washed with pure water' (the outer life purified, perhaps baptism).

The second 'let us' — 'hold fast' (katechomen) 'the profession' (the open confession) of 'our faith,' better 'our hope' (the hope born of and sustained by faith, confessed at baptism, Calvin/Wesley) 'without wavering' (akline, unbending against persecution's pressures); the ground 'he is faithful that promised' (God's covenant fidelity, the confession resting on a God who cannot fail) — perseverance grounded in divine faithfulness.

The third 'let us,' from personal to communal — 'consider one another' (katanoomen, give attentive regard to fellow-Christians' needs and trials) 'to provoke' (eis paroxysmon, our 'paroxysm,' to stimulate/spur, here turned to a good end) 'unto love and to good works'; the redemption of a usually negative word — stirring each other up to love and active goodness, not to anger; the Christian life not solitary, believers responsible for one another's growth.

Mutual care made concrete — 'not forsaking the assembling' (me egkataleipontes, not abandoning the gathered worship, the setting for stirring one another up); 'as the manner of some is' (already some staying away, through fear of persecution, a vain sense of being above ordinances, or drifting toward the synagogue); 'but exhorting one another'; 'so much the more as ye see the day approaching' (judgment or crisis near); forsaking the assembly a step toward apostasy.

The sin defined precisely — 'if we sin wilfully after the knowledge of the truth' means not ordinary sins of infirmity nor recoverable falls but deliberate, total, final apostasy (renouncing Christ after knowing and professing him, the 'drawing back' of v38, Wesley/Henry); 'there remaineth no more sacrifice' — the apostate, rejecting the one sacrifice of Christ, has cast away the sole remedy; not a denial of pardon to the penitent but a warning that apostasy abandons the only ground of forgiveness.

debates.question: What is the 'wilful sin' for which no sacrifice remains?

sides.position: deliberate, total, final apostasy — renouncing Christ and the gospel after knowing the truth, not ordinary sin or a recoverable fall

themes: after the knowledge of the truth

schools.summary: What remains for the apostate — not a sacrifice but 'a fearful looking for of judgment' (ekdoche, a terrified expectation, the conscience anticipating its doom; where Christ's servants look for salvation, v13) and 'fiery indignation which shall devour the adversaries' (the consuming fire of wrath); the present torment of an evil conscience that has tasted and lost grace (Calvin); the apostate become an 'adversary,' falling under the fire reserved for God's enemies.

themes: a fearful looking for of judgment

schools.summary: The lesser case — 'he that despised Moses' law' (athetesas, set it at naught; esp. apostasy/idolatry, Deut 17:2-7) 'died without mercy' (death with no mitigation or pardon) 'under two or three witnesses' (legal justice, not arbitrary, Num 15:30); an argument from the less to the greater (Calvin, a rabbinic rule) — if violating Moses' law brought death without mercy, how much heavier for rejecting the gospel.

The greater guilt — 'how much sorer punishment' (less to greater; not bodily death only but eternal perdition, Calvin) for threefold sin: 'trodden under foot the Son of God' (trampled the Lord of glory with contempt), 'counted the blood of the covenant... an unholy thing' (reckoned the atoning blood common/profane), 'done despite unto the Spirit of grace' (outraged the Spirit who brings grace); describing only the wilful, total apostate (Wesley); the deepest of impieties.

themes: trodden under foot the Son of God
the blood of the covenant unholy
despite unto the Spirit of grace

schools.summary: The warning grounded in God's word — 'we know him that hath said' (the living, true God who keeps his word); 'Vengeance belongeth unto me, I will recompense' (Deut 32:35; Rom 12:19, God claiming the right of retribution) and 'The Lord shall judge his people' (Deut 32:36, judging his own professing people the more rigorously for rebelling against him, Calvin/Wesley); both from the song of Moses, confirming the warning to Jewish believers; no idle threat.

themes: the Lord shall judge his people

schools.summary: The dread conclusion — 'a fearful thing' (phoberon, terrifying, not to be explained away — the wrath of God truly faces the wrongdoer, Robertson) 'to fall into the hands of the living God' (under the avenging power, not for mercy but judgment); 'the living God' not a dead idol but the eternal, almighty God who lives forever and so can punish forever (Clarke); the most fearful of fates, designed to strike awe and keep the wavering from apostasy.

From warning to encouragement (the wise physician soothing after the incision, Chrysostom) — 'call to remembrance the former days' (recalling their fresh early zeal); 'after ye were illuminated' (enlightened with the knowledge of God, brought from darkness to light); 'ye endured a great fight of afflictions' (a hard, prolonged conflict of persecutions soon after believing); their past steadfastness held up to rouse present endurance — shameful to begin well and faint midway (Calvin).

themes: call to remembrance the former days

schools.summary: Two forms of suffering — 'made a gazingstock' (theatrizomenoi, exposed on a stage to derision, 1 Cor 4:9) 'by reproaches and afflictions' (insults and ill-treatment for the faith); and 'companions of them that were so used' (voluntarily sharing others' reproach, standing by the persecuted); the Christian spirit not selfish but pitying and helping the suffering (Henry); to befriend the persecuted, when dangerous, itself a brave confession.

Their joyful sacrifice — 'ye had compassion on' (synepathesate; the better text 'them that were in bonds,' the prisoners for the faith, helped at risk to themselves); 'took joyfully the spoiling of your goods' (accepting the plunder of their property not with grief but joy); the ground 'knowing ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance' (a heavenly inheritance outweighing perishable goods); heavenly hope the secret of cheerful suffering.

themes: took joyfully the spoiling of goods

schools.summary: 'Cast not away' (me apobalete, throw off as worthless) 'your confidence' (parresia, the bold free trust toward God and open profession shown so bravely at first; their liberty of access and birthright as sons, Clarke), 'which hath great recompence of reward' (perseverance richly repaid); confidence the foundation of a godly life, to lose it is to lose the reward (Calvin); having endured much, they must not now surrender the boldness endurance proved.

The present need — 'ye have need of patience' (hypomone, brave endurance, Heb 6:12; not lacking the grace but needing its full exercise in trial); 'that after ye have done the will of God ye might receive the promise' (endurance the bridge between obedience and reward); God furnishing the grace, the believer exercising it (Clarke); patience needed because Satan harasses with countless arts (Calvin); the promise received not at once but after patient continuance.

Encouragement by the Lord's nearness — 'yet a little while' (mikron hoson hoson, doubled, from Isa 26:20, stressing how brief the waiting); 'he that shall come will come, and will not tarry' (Hab 2:3, the coming One given a Messianic application, surely coming, not delaying beyond the appointed time); referring to Christ's coming in judgment on Jerusalem and his final advent; nothing so sustains the fainting as the hope of a near end (Calvin).

themes: he that shall come will come

schools.summary: The great principle and its opposite — 'the just shall live by faith' (Hab 2:4, as in Rom 1:17; the righteous/justified living by faith, the principle and sustenance by which he perseveres; probably 'my righteous one'); 'but if any man draw back' (hypisteiletai, shrink back, apostatize) 'my soul shall have no pleasure in him' (God's abhorrence, the favour forfeited); the alternatives set before the wavering — live by faith and endure, or draw back and be rejected; leading into the faith chapter.

themes: the just shall live by faith

schools.summary: The confident conclusion — emphatic 'we' (against the apostates), the two destinies: 'them who draw back unto perdition' (withdrawing to ruin) vs 'them that believe to the saving of the soul' (holding fast in faith to salvation); 'we are not the cowards, but the courageous' (Clarke); the writer's confident hope that none would apostatize (Barnes), numbering the wavering with the believing; leading into the faith chapter (Heb 11).

themes: not of them who draw back
believe to the saving of the soul
